title: 2026/04/22 - EW [Part 1]
description: Wednesday 22 April 2026 | Education and Workforce Committee Employment Leave Bill

chunks.text: Good morning, Rebecca. We have our first submitter on Zoom. We swap between Zoom and in the room. So we'll leave it to you. We have 10 minutes with you this morning. You are our first submitter on the Employment Leave Bill, so we're pleased to hear from you. And that includes time for questions, which we'll leave over to you to allow time. Over to you.

Thank you. Thank you for having me. I'm Rebecca Graham, and I am the National Director for Parents of Visually Impaired New Zealand. We're a national charity that provides support and advocacy for parents and families of a blind, low vision, deaf blind, or vision impaired child, young person.

Some of our members have adults that they provide care for. The reason we submitted on this bill is a lot of our families have one parent who is the primary caregiving parent, and they work part-time, and they're often juggling part-time work in and around caregiving duties.

And it's a really important role. It's certainly much needed. And we are concerned that this bill actually will penalize our families a little bit. I'm also aware that while we're submitting on behalf of our families, that within the wider disability sector, this is often the case as well, with people providing care for aging parents, and spouses, and other family members.

But certainly our focus is on our parents and our families. With care responsibilities, of course, comes reduced hours, reduced income, and reduced workforce participation. One of the core issues with the bill as proposed is that it ties entitlements and leave entitlements and sick leave entitlements to the number of hours worked.

And this creates a type of systemic disadvantage for our families, and particularly for those who are working part-time hours or who are already working fewer hours because of disability or caregiving related needs. Under the proposed hourly accrual, it means that fewer hours means less annual leave. It means less sick leave.

And for those who are casual workers, it means extremely limited leave. And for casual workers in particular too, there's a loading that gets replaced instead of leave, and it makes it much harder for them to take time off. And that is quite concerning because for our parents, particularly when you're providing care for someone, you do need to be able to juggle things like appointments, things like if the person you're caring for gets sick, being able to take leave to care for them, being able to attend meetings.

I'm not sure if any of you have kids in education, but most of us have what's called IEPs, which is an individual education plan. That happens generally six monthly, and it just about always is in work hours because of course that's when all the other paid professionals can attend. So our parents have to take time off work or rearrange their work schedule to be able to attend.

And those changes, while small, actually do have an impact for our families who are already facing disadvantage. I'm also aware of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, thinking about Articles 27 and 28, which is about employment. And the most recent IMM, International Monitoring Mechanism, report and the concluding observations for New Zealand, which talked about employment and talked about strengthening employment and talked about strengthening the fairness. And that this bill, while it doesn't contradict that, it sort of

undermines it, and it certainly isn't upholding what is being recommended there. The other issue, of course, is the compounding hardship that this bill allows to happen with about 21% of disabled children in New Zealand already live in households experiencing material hardship.

And that is nearly twice as much when compared to non-disabled children. And one of those reasons for that is that the need for someone to be in that caregiving role, they're not being paid the same. And this bill really does undermine some of that really important leave that supports families and supports whanau. It just creates it harder.

It really does. Reducing leave pay to the lowest hourly rate creates a financial disincentive to take leave when sick. We don't want our parents working when they're sick. We want them to be able to rest, to look after themselves. We certainly don't want their health worsening, and we don't want the poorer long-term health outcomes that come when you're unable to take leave and look after yourself.

So, that very quick summary. We do wish to see a retaining of the minimum of four weeks annual leave regardless of hours worked. We do want to maintain universal sick leave entitlements and not have them reduced by hours worked. We do want to retain access to leave for casual workers who demonstrate regular work patterns.

And we also want to ensure that leave continues to accrue during ACC caregiving and return to work periods. We would like to retain current pay calculation methods so that workers are not financially penalized for taking leave. And our concern is that there hasn't been a disability or caregiving impact assessment before progressing this bill, and it would be really good to have that more formally undertaken.And I am going to stop there. So you've got, by my calculation, three minutes for questions. If you have any questions.

If not, that is fine. Thank you, Rebecca. We already have two ready for you, one from Carl Bates. Yeah. Thanks very much, Chair. And look, really appreciate some of the challenges that you've referred to and things that we can look at in the bill process. The bit where I'm a little bit stuck is around the sick leave question. So if you're a parent that works half a day every week on, say, a Monday morning, your contention is that that parent should essentially be able to have 10 weeks in a row sick leave, i.e.

be able to take every Monday morning as sick leave for legitimate reasons maybe, but essentially do that while their colleagues who work full-time wouldn't be able to take 10 weeks sick leave consecutively. And I just can't see where you can argue that that's fair either on other employees or on the employer who has to carry that cost.

And it makes me wonder if actually that's disadvantageous to parents like those that you're referring to who need part-time work, because employers understand the higher cost of having part-time work. That is a hypothetical situation, and I'm not aware that that actually occurs in reality.

I can give you examples of where that happens in reality. So let's not go down that hypothetical debate. Yeah. I can see what you're saying, and that is a challenge for the select committee to work through in terms of how do you uphold that. And I think that's where having a proper disability and caregiving type assessment would be really useful, because that would then give the select committee that kind of economic and expertise which would be able to provide you with actually what is happening here, and what could we do so that

your employees don't feel like someone's taking the mick. But also, equivalently, our parents are able to be supported and take the leave they need. Thank you. Because we do want our parents to be in paid work. That is a huge thing. We would like to support that. Thank you. We've got one more question from Shanan Halbert.

Tena koe, Rebecca. Mihi ana ki a koe te outane. Thanks very much for that submission. I wondered if you could walk us through a real-life example of a family that potentially you've worked with and what these impacts will mean. But secondly, point to an example where the impact assessment has been done in previous work and why that would be beneficial in this instance.

Sure. So what we find with our real-life families is when they don't have flexibility, it's usually the mum, and not always, but it's often the mum, the mum just quits. And this particularly happens when the child ages out of school, because when the child's in school, there's some support there.

Once the child ages out of school, there's nothing for that young person to go into often, and then it's the mum, it's the mum who quits work because they then have these extra demands that they have to juggle, and they can't figure out how to do that if they don't have flexible work. There is some flexible work out there, but if you're thinking about a mum, they've got a kid, they've had to work these part-time casualized hours, they often don't have the luxury of having the types of professional work that offers you the sort of flexibility that some of us enjoy.

And they're the families that I'm most concerned about because they're the ones most at risk. They haven't had the luxury of being able to retrain or be in professional jobs. Thank you. And that creates that type of poverty we're seeing, where someone has to quit work. They're not having access to it. Can I give you- Rebecca, I'm so sorry to interrupt. We have reached the end of your time.

Oh. We have a very, very full day. Look at me blathering on. No, look, it's lovely, and thank you so much for your participation in the process. We really appreciate it. We will need to move on now, so thank you, and have a great day. Thanks. And we have our next submitter all ready on Zoom. We have got Mike Chapman, Chair of New Zealand Ethical Employers Incorporated.

Mike, you've probably heard this all before. Ten minutes, includes time for questions, and we're over to you now. Marina. Our members employ over 20,000 people. A lot of those workers are casual and seasonal workers, so our submission focuses on that. We've got four asks, and I'll go through them very quickly because hopefully our written submission is clear enough.

First is we're suggesting that the committee tighten or clarify the treatment of standard hours, additional hours, and casual hours so that they reflect the work patterns that the people who are employed by our members actually work in. Notional rosters also need, in our second request, to really focus on how our sector works. We're affected by the weather.

We're affected by whether fruit or vegetables are ripe, and also market realities. The market orders may dictate when things can be done. The third request relates to the 12.5% compensation. We're a bit confused about this. So non-permanent workers get 12.5% paid upfront.

Permanent workers don't. The thing about sick leave is it's taken when it's needed. So why would you pay a seasonal worker, a casual worker, 12.5% at the start before they've taken any sick leave, whereas your permanent worker is in a different situation, where you assess that sick leave is taken out of the 12.5%?Yeah, the bill's doing a fantastic thing. It's clarifying an area which has been a massive problem in terms of just trying to do things right, and so we really support that.

And therefore, our fourth request is we're really interested, when this bill becomes law, that really good worked-up examples are provided by MBIE, so that employers and workers can make the bill work and make it work well. Thank you. Thank you very much, Mike. That's incredibly helpful and constructive.

Do I have any questions from members? If not, can I say, Mike, that is not a lack of interest. That is a very comprehensive and constructive submission, so really appreciate it. Thank you, and I can see our officials writing feverishly in the background. So, we'll give you a few minutes back in your day. Thank you very much. Thanks for your time.

Thanks so much, Mike. Cheers, Mike. Cheers. Bye. Right. Our next submitter, we have the New Zealand Airline Pilots Association, I think in the room. Wonderful. Welcome, Imogen. You've heard it all before. You've got 10 minutes. We don't need to rush you to the table.

Thank you. And so that 10 minutes includes time for questions, so if you can leave some time, that'd be great. Awesome. Thank you. Obviously here to represent NZALPA as a part of our legal team. So we look after 83 different employer groups, and we advocate for fair employment conditions, safety, wellbeing, and operational integrity within New Zealand's highly regulated aviation sector.

So this submission today, obviously we'd like to discuss our concerns about the interaction with aviation's unique operational realities, including irregular hours, complex rostering, and fatigue management obligations. NZALPA would like to see sector-specific exemptions and continued collaboration to ensure the bill promotes clarity and fairness without undermining aviation safety, collective bargaining outcomes, and operational stability for our regulators.

In terms of talking about each of our sort of big-ticket items separately, the categories of working hours is one of our biggest issues that we are currently looking at with the proposed bill. The introduction of standard, additional, and casual hour classifications represents significant departure from our existing conditions. Aviation rostering is highly complex and demand driven.

No working shift or rostering pattern is completely the same. We've got weather constraints, we've got operational demands, flight cancellations, the current constraints that we're facing with fuel prices and the economy. We've got a lot of demands to look at in that space. One of our main concerns was the notional rostering pattern, and if we were looking at how the labor inspectorate would apply the 13-week period, there is no particular 13 weeks that would actually look the same no matter what combination that you could actually draw from

the rostering patterns. So in terms of from an equity perspective, we would like to sort of understand a little bit more about whether or not there would be any exemptions in terms of application in that space or how that would particularly work. So that's one of the big items for us. And in terms of talking about annual leave and sick leave accruals all together at the same time, given the fact that we do have varying different types of shifts, when we're looking at standby callbacks, there's no particular hours implemented. We would like to understand how

we would apply that in terms of a regular shift pattern. It's very different for every different employee. We also, when we're looking at the sick leave pro-proration, a lot of our employees are actually part-time, and we have sometimes three to five different categorizations of part-time employees across our different collective agreements. So when we're looking at how the part-time piece would work, we would see, in terms of the fatigue management piece, a lot of our part-time employees being significantly impacted within the aviation

side and within other public services. Obviously, heightened sick leave is a common thing every day. With the bereavement leave and the family violence leave part day entitlements, at the moment it's near impossible for us to ensure our employees to have part day shifts given the fact that they are shifts that can't be disrupted 9 times out of 10. So it'd be very hard for us to see part day entitlements to be enacted.

The otherwise working day test, if the statutory 50% test overrides bargain provisions, members risk indirect disadvantage, loss of certainty, increased disputes, and compliance uncertainty. Leave payments, what we're sort of looking at as another big-ticket item is the reconciliation piece. Given that we have obviously different rostering constraints, if we were to agree to some form of notional roster, we would have to see, obviously, the next pay period or agreements between the parties of the collective agreements to agree to a reconciliation

period, which would actually be really hard for us. And we've sort of projected that the wage arrears claims would go through the roof in our sector, given if we can't manage how these reconciliation periods are, and we would rely on employees coming forward to us consistently to point out that their leave accruals haven't been administered correctly.

The proposed leave compensation payment of 12.5% may be appropriate for genuine casual employment but represents a concerning shift for permanent employees. Under the bill, additional hours worked beyond standard hours would no longer enhance leave entitlements. In aviation, variable and extended hours are inherent to permanent employment.

The approach effectively recharacterizes ordinary work as quasi-casual, erodes leave value over time, and risks undermining fatigue and safety frameworks.When we talk about the remediation of historical non-compliance, given that the vast majority of our employer groups are shaped by the current Holidays Leave Act provisions, and given the complex rostering, current sort of remediation in terms of rest and meal breaks, arrears claims, and things like that, we do rely on the calculations that are going through the Holidays Act. And for us to sort of move

away from the current precedents in place would create a huge ripple effect through all of our collective agreements with how rostering works and how leave will be calculated. To sort of conclude, our sort of big-ticket items are we really would like to see greater flexibility in terms of the classification of working hours, to recommend to become aligned with the operational realities of aviation work, and that no day is the same.

Support is given to obviously cashing up 25% of annual leave entitlements, as long as it obviously remains in that voluntary space. Standard leave entitlements and accrual mechanisms, we'd like to see some form of exemption in that space, given that we can't see there being any certainty around the sick leave and annual leave accruals from an equity perspective, given that all of the roster periods derived are completely different. And we would have to negotiate on a notional roster that we could have set in place, but the

realized hours would automatically create a reconciliation every single time. So we'd like to obviously stick with the status quo. A clear and adaptable definition of part-day leave should be included in the legislation, allowing mutual agreement on its application within operational context. The statutory otherwise working day test should operate as a default mechanism to avoid overriding collectively bargained provisions, with contractual provisions prevailing if they're more preferable. Leave payment calculations should reflect actual

earnings, including variable components, to preserve fairness and economic protection for employees with fluctuating income patterns. A hybrid model for alternative holiday entitlements is recommended, allowing hourly accruals to be into full days' worth of leave, with a minimum entitlement floor for employees working on public holidays. And clearly for something both employees and employers would like, flexibility in terms of pay statements.

We have sort of anticipated a huge reconciliation period that would happen. And obviously, sector-specific accommodations are necessary for the remediation of historical non-compliance. At the end of the day, we do just have considerable concerns around the rostering constraints and what that's going to mean for accruals and correct application.

Does anyone have any questions? Yes. Thank you. So we have three questions lined up-... and not a lot of time, so can I ask members to be brief with the kind of question so that we can get a brief answer? Thank you. Carl first. Thanks, Chair. In section 8, you talk about the cashing up. Yes. And saying that's a good thing. And then in section 10, you talk about the need for genuine rest and recreation.

Yes. And those two concepts just challenge each other. So I was interested in your thought. Yes. I think from an employee engagement perspective, we're happy to obviously allow for... We don't promote it within aviation to cash up your annual leave. We're more saying we support the cashing-up request if it remains autonomous in terms of you can select what you cash up, and there's mutual agreement in that space. But we do, our position is to promote people to take annual leave and use it for rest and recreation.

That's why one of our main oppositions was only being able to take obviously one period of 14-day leave outside of mutual agreement within the anniversary period. So yeah, we do obviously support completely people taking leave. Thanks. Yeah. Parmjeet Parmar.

Yeah, thank you. So about the part-day leave- Yes... you have expressed your concerns about that. So what happens in a situation when somebody's on a shift and falls sick? So in that situation, the person- Yeah... will have to go, and so you already might be managing that, isn't it, in a way? Yeah. So it is a current obviously reality that we face, and that's why sometimes we have flight cancellations and things like that, or well, it happens all the time. So if someone is obviously sick or fatigued, they would have to remove

themselves from their shift, and then they have to either call in someone from standby or callback sort of component, or there's someone ready to go. In that instance, obviously, that is a part-day mechanism that would potentially come into place, but obviously, it's an actual full day worth of leave taken dependent on the collective agreement. So there are some benefits, I do acknowledge in that sense.

But in terms of the application, calling up in the morning, "I've got to take my child to an appointment. Can I have a half day?" It's a full shift realized if that- Yeah... answers that question. Yeah. Thank you. And then a final question from Shannon Halbert. Thanks so much. And hey, the sector's done a lot of work around the fatigue management framework- Mm-hmm... over a few years. And in hearing your summary today, that this really is counter to what the sector's been trying to achieve in that space.

Is that your understanding, and if so, why? I think in terms of the accrual rates, like if we're looking at sick leave and rostering constraints in itself, in terms of the fatigue management perspective piece, I would say the sick leave proration would be our main concern in terms of fatigue management. And that's purely because obviously, if you're reducing someone's leave entitlements, they're more likely to go to work feeling unwell.

Mm. And given that aviation professionals quite often get unwell, given the sector that they work in and the hours that they work, and the fatigue they do face every day, reducing their entitlement, while we don't have the statistics, but I could imagine that it would increase sick leave through the roof and risk to the public sector, given people might choose to operate in an unsafe environment.

Thank you. Yeah. Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Really appreciate your submission, being part of the process. Thank you. Nice to have someone in person sometimes. I know. Thank you. Awesome. All right. We move on now to Horticulture New Zealand. Sarah Cameron and Dean Smith, welcome.You're online. We have 10 minutes with you. That includes time for questions, and I'll leave you to manage that. And it's over to you now.

So good morning, everybody. There are two issues of concern for us noted in our submission, which is the LCP payment and the otherwise working day test. And we mirror Mike Chapman's comments around the LCP payment. So fully aware we've only got a short time for questions, so I'll just quickly hand over to Dean, who will have some comments to make on both of those.

Kia ora, everyone. Thanks, Sarah. Look, I suppose from a Summerfruit NZ perspective, we obviously represent growers of cherries, peaches, plums, apricots, nectarines in New Zealand. Our primary concern is especially for the cherry industry, and I think we need to consider the dynamics of that industry. So, it's an industry which has a very compressed season, so starts around December, finishes in early February. So it's normally around a five- to six-week sprint.

So it's an industry that relies very, very heavily on casual labor, and I guess the context around that is the material impact that the proposed changes would have on the profitability of that industry by baking in sick leave entitlements into the LCP. So for context, around 40% of total operating expenditure for an average cherry orchard is casual labor. So, by baking in an additional 4.5% sick leave on 40% of your opex, that's effectively a 2% increase in total expense.

And when you're operating with gross margins in the vicinity of 10% in an average year, this year won't be anything like that. Obviously, effectively that's 2% of your profit gone by baking this in. And I think the other thing, we acknowledge the importance of sick leave, but I guess by baking it in from day one and making it effectively an additional fixed cost, that's a major concern.

I guess the other aspect of this is around the otherwise working day aspect. The cherry industry is also unique in that it has an unavoidable timeframe, which coincides with a significant number of public holidays. So effectively, you're looking at around 8% to 9% of working days are public holidays, which are obviously unavoidable.

And we don't feel that that's equitable. It's not currently, and the bill doesn't resolve it. In the submission, you'll see that we believe a more equitable approach would be to deal with that by adding an additional margin on top of the casual wage. Sarah, you might want to elaborate on what's proposed.

Yeah. So we're proposing a 4% across the board payment. And I do have a point of clarification around that. In the submission that was consulted on throughout the industry, that 4% is for an eight-week period. So for casual workers that are employed less than eight weeks, that 4% would apply to account for those condensed public holidays over the Christmas period for the cherry industry, not for casual workers, no matter how long they are employed.

So we've based the calculation on the number of public holidays there are per year versus the 260 working days, which is noted in the Cabinet paper. And I guess, yeah, we invite questions now. Wonderful. Thank you. I do have a question myself. I understand the difference with the casual nature, or the seasonal nature of your casual employment.

When it comes to your perspective, I suppose, on the changes, I just want to understand, I think, what sort of preparation there is for any change that ever could happen. Any change in this particular case, and I really understand everything that you're coming through with.

In terms of the 2% increase, we've seen RSE changes over time, how the sector's dealt with that and cost implications and sort of the preparation for things like this that might happen. Yeah, look, I guess we appreciate that inflation happens, and that you have to have a business model that can absorb inflation.

I guess this just comes on top of everything else, and the materiality of it for this part of the industry is probably the thing that's the most alarming. As I say, we have a disproportional reliance on casual labor, and it's unavoidable, and the casual people that we are calling on are probably perhaps not the people that were perceived to be who the changes were to consider, in that we are talking about backpackers, we're talking about students.

These are the people that we rely on to harvest cherries at this peak time. And so for context, you might have an operator that might have four or five permanent staff, but their workforce might swell to 50 or 60 backpackers and students that they rely on at that time. So we can't get away from the impact of it, and I guess it's the materiality of the impact on profitability which we are really wanting to highlight, I suppose.

If I said to you that, for instance, if your gross margin's 10% and 2%'s taken away, that's a 20% reduction in your profit. If your boss rocked up to you and said, "Hey, by the way, your salary's going to increase by 20%," I don't think you'd consider that immaterial. Yeah. And thank you. That's- And if I could just-- Sorry. If I could- Yeah, no, go... yeah, a comment around, you raised the RSE scheme, and we are in a situation now with that scheme where it has become-Probably not economically viable for employers to continue using it, and we're seeing big drop-offs

in the numbers of employers utilizing the scheme because of the financial burden attached to it around the pastoral care. So, employers are really facing it out there, and they're having to make choices which impact their bottom line. Thank you. That's incredibly helpful context to especially the earlier part of the question.

It doesn't appear that we have any further questions, but can I please say that it's not a lack of interest. You have put forward a very, very helpful and comprehensive submission, and I know our officials will really appreciate it. Awesome. So thank you for that. Thanks. Thank you. Thanks for the opportunity. Thank you for being part of the process. Yeah, thank you very much. All right, bye. Bye.

All right. We have Access Community Health Limited, and we are running a little early now, so my hope is that they are available. Wonderful. We have got Warwick Jackson joining us online. Good morning. Good morning, Warwick. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions, so we'll leave you to manage that, and hand over to you now.

Yeah. Tena koe chair, members of the committee. Thank you for the opportunity to represent Access Community Health regarding the Employment Leave Bill. I intend to only speak for five minutes and leave time for questions. My name is Warwick Jackson. I am the Executive Director, Strategy and Finance for Access. Access Community Health is a New Zealand-based home and community organization. We help tens of thousands of people every week live their lives at home every day. To do this, we have 3,500 amazing staff. This includes nurses,

people who work in the office, but also 3,000 support workers. So support workers are quite a unique group. They mostly work part-time, maybe 20 to 30 hours a week on average. Their work often changes because the people we support have changing needs. They move in and out of our service.

So rosters change weekly, week on week, daily. Hours change, frequency changes. It's very difficult to manage. With all that complexity and volume, all the edge cases come up commonly for our sort of business. So any sort of scenario that you think, "Oh, that might be a bit unlikely," we probably get it. So that's why this bill is so important to us.

So firstly, I say we support the bill, and it's moved from a weeks-based system to an hours accrual system. However, I want to focus on one particular part of our submission that the bill, as written, has the potential to create a perverse incentive for employees to at times not take their sick leave and to not agree to standard hours.

A sick health worker should not be working, as they put the health of themselves and the vulnerable people we care for at risk. The most important change we are looking for is to either remove the LCP payment system altogether for non-casual staff or allow it to be optional so leave can be accrued on all hours worked.

As an example to illustrate this, a support worker often begins with very few hours, maybe five a week, and they build up their hours if they want to. And let's say they build up those hours to 40 hours per week. So in the extreme, that person has got five standard hours and 35 additional hours.

So that's 35 hours that they're getting paid LCP for, the 12.5%. When the employer proposes converting those hours from additional hours to standard hours to better affect the worker's ongoing schedule, the employee faces a rational economic choice to refuse this. Converting would mean losing the immediate LCP payment because they're getting more. They're getting it in their pocket.

They don't have to wait for it. Yes, they're exchanging it for not accruing leave, but in terms of that choice for that week, you're effectively decreasing your pay. So where the worker is encouraged to not accept standard hours and the employer has a need for hours, this puts the employer in a precarious position. And then when that worker is sick, they are faced with some unintended choices.

They may take sick leave and get paid sick leave for the standard and additional hours in their roster. So in that example, they had one standard hour for the day and seven additional hours, so they'll get paid their eight hours plus LCP, so they actually get paid more for seven of those hours. But with working just five standard hours a week, they're only accruing 10 hours sick leave a year. So on that day, they're using eight out of their 10 hours. That's 80% for one day sick leave. So they're left with a difficult choice

about whether to take leave or not. Mm. In another scenario, maybe the sick leave is planned. Maybe they have an operation coming up, and at the time of booking, their roster might not be full, so they might just have the one standard hour in there. This is common for us. They are now incentivized to not tell their employer that they're going to have an operation, to wait until their roster is filled up, and maybe even to ask for more work because an LCP payment is higher than a standard out sick leave payment, thus putting the care of others at risk.

Now, we expect our support workers to not do this, of course, but we would also expect a law to not incentivize people to do it. These scenarios would be solved very simply if leave could be accrued on all hours worked. The worker would have sick leave to use and would not need to consider the current state of their roster before booking sick leave.

We then move on to determining how many hours they're going to get paid, which is an important part of the same issue.Given how frequently work changes, hours and days, the employment agreement roster and notional roster are all not very practical frameworks for us. A notional roster, when somebody's hours are changing every week, would have to be agreed every week.

That's just completely impractical. So we would like to be able to choose to use the 13-week average to determine a notional roster to be used for leave. Legislation already allows this method to be used by the labor inspector, and as such, we would like to be able to choose to use that as a method. So, for example, if the support worker works on average five hours a week on a Tuesday and they're sick, they can be paid five hours.

Thank you. Any questions? Thank you, Warwick. That's an incredibly helpful contextual submission. We have one question from Kamjit Tamar. A very quick one, Warwick. Thank you so much. So, you don't like leave compensation payment, and you have explained the reasons about how employees can have their views towards employment agreements and towards shift work. But is there anything else in the system that will create any complexity for you in terms of basically calculating the pay, tracking it, payroll systems?

Do you see any complexity there? Yes. So wherever there's a switch or an if in the legislation, that creates complexity for us. So, for example, if there's going from your agreement hours to your roster hours to your notional hours, building all that sort of thing into a system is expensive and complicated, and that's one of the reasons we'd like to just be able to choose the 13-week average.

The switch from standard hours to additional hours, that creates complexity. Again, something that needs a rule, that needs tracking, that needs updated agreements, that needs the employee to agree to it. That's extremely cumbersome when you have a large workforce whose hours change regularly. Does that answer your question?

Yeah, no, thanks for that. But that would happen in any employment situation anyway, isn't it? Because actual hours worked might vary from what's in the contract. Was the question, is that happening now, and how do we deal with it now? Yeah. Like now, also. Yes. So right now in the legislation, there's a concept of definition of a week.

The current legislation doesn't define what a definition of a week is, so we actually have in our agreements, or some of the people in our industry have in their agreements, that the definition of a week is a rolling average. Shall I start, Tamara? I hesitate to try and explain what's in somebody else's heart, but the effect of this legislation is to funnel wealth upwards and away from working people. That's the effect of it. I can only speculate on what the motivation might be, and I think that I would ask the select committee to ask who is being served here. As the CTU said, actually, it's not going to make employers'

lives easier, but it may save them some money. Tamara? Why do I think this government is focusing on this? Because this government doesn't give a damn about the average working person in New Zealand. They've screwed us over already. They're just trying to do it again and to a deeper- Christian... level.

Kia ora. We have a question from Carl Bates. Thanks very much, Chair. There's been a lot of focus on this bill on rest and recreation. Yes. For members of your union who have a primary job and then they have a secondary job in order to earn more money for whatever purposes they choose for that money to be used for, does your union charge them fees on both jobs?

We have a fee structure that depends on the hours that people work. That's our current fee structure. So when we talk about employers only being interested in money, does that apply to the union's financial model for membership as well? Absolutely not. We invest pretty much every cent of members' money in organizing and empowering working people for a better life, and our submission here is part of our aim to create better lives for working people. So kia ora for the question.

Thank you very much for your participation in this process, Rachel and Tamara. We do appreciate it. We'll move on now to our next submitter. Now we have got the New Zealand Dairy Workers Union. Chris Flash, I believe we have you online. It'll be a good one.

Kia ora. Welcome, Chris. We have 10 minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions, and we will leave it with you to manage that, and it's now your time. Kia ora, thanks. Tēnā koutou katoa. Ko Chris Flash tōku ingoa. Thanks for this opportunity to present to the committee on this bill.

I want to acknowledge the other submitters that have made important points, and also in our written submissions support those. I wanted to highlight today quite a critical issue for us in the dairy industry that you may be aware of this, you may not be, that we think there's some serious implications for dairy processing hidden in this bill.

In particular, it relates to annualised hour system. We talk a bit about this in our written submission. I'll take that as read, and I don't really want to go into chapter and verse into that, but it is important. Probably a number of you have not been around dairy processing sites or other related sites and the use of annualised hours. It is a very complicated system that's highly used across Fonterra, all food ingredients, which is the OFI group in Tokoroa, Miraka, Melody Dairies, and not just in dairy processing.

We are aware that Downer's Construction and other groups use annualised hours. Why is it so critical? Annualised hours effectively takes a seasonal product, be it dairy, and turns that into a full-time role for people. And we don't have this challenge that we have in so many other parts of the primary industry of, yes, we're never going to change the seasonal nature of the product, but not having workforces that are tuned and burned through seasonal hire and fire processes, and annualised hours is a critical component

that we have used in the dairy processing industry for many years to achieve that. And as you'll see from the economic results in dairy processing and the role that dairy plays in the economy, I would put some serious warning into all members, particularly National Party members who, in your own electorates, could have some very unintended consequences around this proposed change. Particularly, it's around the accruing instead of allocating annual leave.

That's going to severely disrupt those annualised hours arrangements. We have a system that allows annual leave hours to be accrued as you go. Workers perform those standard hours, and the act will take away the ability to receive an allocation of annual holidays at the beginning of the season and plan and use those holidays during their annualised hours salary year. The loss of planning for the season is to the detriment of both workers and dairy employers.

We're in discussions with Fonterra around this. We are aware that Fonterra and a number of employers have submitted. We feel that there is some very serious issues around the calculation. Now, I know in 10 minutes we probably don't have time to delve into that. That's not probably the time to go into all the technical areas around how annualised hours are going to be impacted.

But we would say that one of the key issues that we need to see, first is this bill actually being removed completely. But if it isn't going to be removed, we would ask for some very clear exclusion clauses for the protection of collective agreements. Particularly part 2 of Schedule 1 of the bill at Item 6 regarding provisions of employment agreements to comply with the Act should be reworded to give unions the option to exclude collective agreements from the compliance requirements of the transitional arrangements and be followed by exclusion clauses which

allow the option for collective agreement arrangements to stay in place as status quo to be determined by the relevant unionWe must have holiday leave legislation that properly accounts the extensive range of shift patterns and annualised hours arrangements in multiple collective agreements in the dairy industry. These annualised hour arrangements allow us to deliver these intensified work during the season and especially during the flush, but then effectively workers have banked those hours.

It gives the ability for workers and employers under annualised hours to work through those intense periods, banking hours, building them up, and so then in the off season, they don't effectively have to be made redundant or take time off. They're actually paid and build that payment back for working through those hours.

So in order to meet annualised hours' needs, many collective agreements that the DW are party to rely on that current Section 17 of the Holidays Act, which is how employees' entitlement to annual holidays may be met, which states that an employer and employee may agree on how an employee's entitlement to four weeks annual holidays is to be met based on what genuinely constitutes a working week for the employee.

We would submit in the case the bill becomes law, that current Holiday Act provision should be brought over to any new legislation to preserve Holiday Act arrangements to provide for that flush. This is a highly technical area, and I do know that Fonterra has sought an extension of transitional arrangements. We would support that.

I think this is an area that does need to be unpacked. We would submit again that the essence of the bill is not needed, but if you are to proceed with this, there is some very serious issues in relation to the operation of annualised hours in a highly profitable, highly effective, and highly productive industry. That's not the case in many other industries in the primary sector, and I would ask the select committee to take some time to delve into this issue, because there could be quite a few unintended consequences for

one of New Zealand's most important, productive, and economically successful sectors. I know I've taken far too much time talking, and you'll want to ask questions, so I'll shut up now and hand over to the committee. Thank you, Chris. Actually, you've given us ample time. I have a question here from Carl Bates. Thanks very much. And thanks, Chris, for the submission.

I'm putting aside, if I can call it the boilerplate comment from a union, that we should just not proceed with the bill. As the member of Parliament for Whanganui, which includes, obviously, Hawera and Stratford, both important areas that you reference, appreciate this challenge that you've raised- Yeah... and would obviously be more than happy to meet with local members in relation to this.

But from a practical solution perspective, rather than simply have a leave collective agreements alone, because that assumes that unions across all industries have this right, and I just think that that is not a realistic statement to make. Would there be, or would it be appropriate to have some form of industry carve-out that the minister or the ministry was able to determine on appropriate cases like the one that you've described, instead of simply saying, "As unions, we've got this right"?

Look, to answer the question, first and foremost, I would say that in relation to the dairy industry, and I reiterate our submission, this isn't an area where we have low union involvement. The Dairy Workers Union and other related unions such as E tū and UA, we cover over 90, 95% of all workers in this industry. So in dairy processing, we have a very long and proud history of over 85 years of engagement.

So in any discussions around that, that's why we say the collective agreement clauses in this industry should be adhered to and honored. So in that dairy industry. It's not like we're a small player that sits on the edge. And this is a standard term that many new players, such as Allyn Food Ingredients, Medaka, Melody Dairies, .

But I think the key word there was, in this industry. Medaka. Yeah. And that's the point. And so I think we're agreed on that point. Yeah. That it's not across the board, all collective agreements in every industry. You've actually made the point, which is in this industry. So thanks . Yep. And in terms of the second point, we would be open for discussions about where there is related industries, because you may be aware in construction, there is similar terms as well. So there's a need to be looking at how annualised

hours needs to be discussed. Can I-- Oh, yeah. Go for it, Grant. You go for it. So obviously one of the reasons for the whole change in this bill coming forward was the level of complexity that people have been struggling with for quite some time. You clearly have not had that issue in the dairy sector at all.

It's a highly complicated area, but we've been able to, over decades of work with annualised hours and across many employers, find a solution that we feel, it needs ongoing work, but a solution that provides our seasonal industry with full-time, permanent labor jobs.

And I want to really go to this point because this is what we're struggling in so much of the primary sector, where we have sectors such as pip fruit, horticulture, forestry, and chain and burn, where people come in for a small season. Even red meat, they come in for a while and then they churn out at the end of the season. Annualised hours is a critical component, and it is technical and it's complicated, but it has quite a complicated interaction with the Holidays Act because it provides full-time employment across a whole season.

And in that off-season, whanau, families, workers don't have to uproot and move, as you know, out to other industries or other parts of the country. It's a critical model. We'd like to see this model actually applied across many other parts of the primary sector. We think it's got real ability to solve many of our problems and productivity issues across the primary sector.

And so I would just challenge that in terms of this interaction, there could be quite a nuanced interaction between annualised hours and the proposed Holidays Act amendments. So we'd like to have that opportunity maybe to take that offline and delve into that further. It is still complicated. The Holidays Act has issues, no doubt about that.

But the underlying assumption in this bill is the removal of work rights, and we do not believe that that is inherently the right way to approach this matter. As a dairy farmer, I appreciate your comments. Thank you. Thanks. Thank you, Chris, for your submission. We'll leave it there. We've reached the end of our time with you.

Appreciate your submission and being part of our select committee process, as usual. Thank you. We'll move on now to Toi Matahora. We have Steve Huring joining us in the room. Ah. It's refreshing. Welcome, Steve. Well, I thought there'd be more here in person, but no. It's a new age.

It is a new age, indeed. Welcome. I'm sorry, I don't have the names of your- Oh... colleagues, but I'm sure you can introduce yourself. We've got time for you. Ten minutes includes time for questions, and we'll hand it over to you now. Okay. Thanks for that. So I'm Steve Huring, the industrial director at ASMS. With me is Virginia Mills, our senior policy advisor, and James Roberts, our policy and research advisor.

So as you probably know, we are the union representing senior doctors and dentists, the consultants. We have more than 6,000 members across the Ministry of Health, Te Whatu Ora, Health New Zealand, the Accident Compensation Corporation, and many other smaller healthcare providers. Okay, our main issue, as has been highlighted already, is this bill will lead to less leave for lots and lots of workers who currently have guaranteed access to full public holidays and a full four weeks annual leave at the moment.

And more corrosively, it will lead to less leave pay. And so by our calculations, there's going to be a massive transfer of millions and millions of dollars from employees to employers as a result of this bill. And that is because it effectively discounts any rate other than the standard ordinary hourly rate for lots of hours. The complexity is mind-boggling.

One of the great features of the current act is that it can account for a diverse number of employment relationships. Just heard about the dairy workers with annualized hours. All our doctors have to do call requirements. In other words, they have to take part in a roster in their departments and do a certain amount of hours overnight on call.

Now, those hours are paid at T 1.5. This bill basically strips the 0.5 off the T and turns that into T1. So for every doctor, effectively when they take leave, there's going to be quite a large reduction in pay. And those call requirements are quite oppressive and onerous.

Lots of doctors try and get off call as they get older, but it's just one of the things that makes the health system work, and this is going to completely disincentivize people doing call. And so by our calculations, just on that calculation alone, for 6,000-odd doctors, it's in the order of $15 million being transferred from employees to the employer.

The complexity is a real serious issue. So at the moment, everyone gets a week. You can calculate what a week is just by ordinary weekly pay or average weekly earnings. This requires really, really complex accounting for standard hours, additional hours. Which type of payment did you get?

Was it an event-based payment? Was it an hourly-based payment? Was it a payment with penal rates? There's a complexity in this that as we were writing our submission, we even struggled with. So it's actually moved, in our view, from a relatively simple system to a very, very complex system. Loss of public holidays, that's a concern, as is the four weeks, as was raised by other unions. We've got a particular concern about clause 17, multiple roles.

It's a very strange thing. It's got no corresponding thing in the current Public Holidays Act. It appears to be inserted to deal with a case that we won in the Employment Relations Authority, which just basically says you can't take leave if you've got different leave balances and different roles with the same employer.

So, we've had an anesthetist who also worked in the ICU just down the corridor, four doors away at Auckland Hospital. To be more flexible to the employer, they decided to drop their hours in one role and pick up hours in the other, so they were split between the two. They started with a zero leave balance in the new role. Could they use any of their four weeks leave in their primary role? No, they couldn't.

Couldn't take a whole week off because they were split in split roles. It's an administrative nightmare that was particular to Auckland Hospital, isn't practiced in any other DHB in the country, and appears to have been put up, we believe, by Health New Zealand to actually just overturn that employment decision.

So that's a great concern. Rushed in, we believe. What is the role? And, as we say in our submission, we think it will count against the employer's ability to use their employees flexibly as well. Now, you will all have heard of occasions where production line A slows down. It would be great if employees could take leave from that role and maybe pick up something else.

But no, this bill will basically say, "Hey, your leave balances are siloed to a particular role in a particular factory or a particular organization." So you're taking away the flexibility for the employer as well.

We have a big issue about the incentivization of casualization. At the moment, we've got in the health system, lots of people, not necessarily our members, but lots of people who you would describe as permanent casuals.But they get to have leave, and their permanent casual status gives both the employer and the employee quite a lot of flexibility to fill in gaps in the health system.

There are a lot of gaps in the health system. At the moment, those people get leave. They will be designated as casuals under the system. They won't get leave. They'll get compensation instead. And I just think this is a backward step against the objects of the act of providing universal leave for everyone.

Picking up on the dairy workers, what we call is an undue limitation on freedom to contract. So this bill repeats the existing legislation that says if you contract for any provisions that are better than the act, you're entitled to have those. Freedom of negotiation, fundamental right. But then the bill goes on to say later in transitional arrangements, but whatever you negotiate will have to abide by the structure, the complex structure of this bill, and you will have to move to an hourly-based accrual system. And I just don't think that

those two concepts sit side by side. Freedom to negotiate better conditions, just like the dairy workers have, just like arguably we have in our union, and then be confined to basically applying a very strict, what I would describe as a system that's designed for the gig economy, where basically everyone's treated like an hourly worker.

So, I just think there's lots and lots-- I think the drafters of this bill have not taken into account the diversity of employment relationships in the country. Stock workers who come in from the bush for two weeks and go back in for two weeks, fishermen who go to sea and then have a certain amount of time off, dairy workers, whatever, doctors doing call at night, it just hasn't accounted for that.

And at the moment, we've got a system that we can actually work with. As was pointed out, Section 17 allows us to enter into negotiation about what constitutes a working week. There's been very little litigation on Section 17 of the act about what is a working week. So we would regard that as an indication that things are working.

I think I'll leave it at that and welcome any questions. Great. I have a question for you, a little provocative. What came first, the chicken or the egg? Because the reason that we're talking about the issues today are a result of, I think, a long period of time where employers have adapted to the Holidays Act.

Everything that you're talking about, my understanding, and correct me if I'm wrong, is a result of working with the Holidays Act. So I'm assuming you've taken the Holidays Act and worked that into your collective agreements with your employees. Would you not think that over a period of time you would then adapt to this piece of legislation and build that then into your collective agreements, and then by proxy, employers that don't have staff in unions or with collective agreements would then say, "Right, this helps

us be more flexible, and therefore we will then arrange our staff allotment," whether it's how they put someone from one space into another space in their employment as a result of the changes here? Well, I think the bill has evolved since 1945, and then, the Factories Act before that, and it's evolved in a way that, as I said, has had very little litigation about how people have worked.

Firefighters who work two weeks on, two weeks off basically have worked out a definition of leave that conforms with the Holidays Act, but they can't move to that with their shift systems. They can't move to this system. I just think this system-- Well, if you're implying that this system should start redefining employment relationships, I think that's the wrong way around.

The Holidays Act should basically be trying to provide paid leave for all sorts of employment relationships. It shouldn't be then basically putting everyone in a straitjacket and saying, "Hey, you need to change." We can't change necessarily the way doctors do call. You go on call, you're on availability provision.

You can be in there all night, or you can be in there for just an hour, and why would we change that around to fit into some sort of administrative thing? I do believe this has been driven by the payroll companies. Business New Zealand, the CTU, and MBIE basically had a consensus about what needed to be changed in the Holidays Act, and then, as we know, there was a very sudden change.

What we do know is there was massive lobbying by payroll companies, and the payroll companies are actually responsible for some of the cock-ups in Holidays Act remediation. They basically built systems that didn't do what the Holidays Act was supposed to do. Millions have been spent in the health system fixing those up. They are now fixed, they are now working, and they're actually calculating leave correctly, and payroll companies have got off scot-free, I think, in their responsibility for that.

Now the payroll companies have come back and said, "Hey, we want to revert to the system that was wrong in the first place, that we've spent a lot of money fixing up." Mm-hmm. "We'd actually like to revert back to those very simple systems, and then try and drive employment relationships and employment arrangements to fit our simplistic hour-based legislation," which is not simple.

Three categories of hours, five categories of income that you have to calculate of which goes where. It's going to lead to masses of litigation. There's a long answer to your short question. We have a quick question from Vanessa Wayne. Great answer. Thank you very much. The current system that is in place allows for quite a lot of variability and individual arrangements in turn with the collective agreements.

Do you see that it is problematic in any way? No, I don't. Look, the industry is full of quite interesting shift systems, 12 hours on, 12 hours off, like a paper mill or whatever. And those shift systems, and those rotating shift systems don't accord exactly with a seven-day cycle.

But they've easily come up with definitions of a week so that they can take their leave and not disrupt the shift cycle. So in other words, they've basically come up with definitions of a week that fit into those shift rosters. So you take your leave, just like the firefighters do when you're off work.

Rather than when you're shift working. And you guarantee to be on the job, not take leave when you're on the shift. Mm-hmm. That's absolutely through the industry, and this is going to overturn all that. This just basically says you can take an hour off whenever you like, and you'll accrue on hours. And it's not going to conform- It's not... to people getting the equivalent of four weeks off.

Okay. Thank you very much. We've reached the end- Thank you... of our time with you. Appreciate your contribution to our process. Thank you.
